paper_title: \boldmath Comparison of Silvaco and Synopsys TCAD Predictions Including the Perugia Radiation Damage Model in Silicon Pixel Detectors for the HL-LHC

P000_S0000.quote: the frequency $f$ was iteratively adjusted until the CV characteristics of Synopsys and Silvaco matched
P000_S0000.reason: The surrounding context states a fixed frequency f=1 kHz was used; the perturbed text falsely claims f was iteratively adjusted to match results, a post-hoc selection flaw contradicting the fixed-parameter methodology.

P001_S0001.quote: interface traps have no physical impact on the surface damage model for unirradiated sensors
P001_S0001.reason: The original text says interface traps were excluded from the simulation setup; the perturbed version makes a physical claim about their impact that is not supported and misrepresents the reason for their exclusion.
P001_S0001.original:
In figure~\ref{fig:unirr} the main results from the simulation of the unirradiated device are presented. The diode was simulated at $T = $~300~K, including only interface charges and excluding interface traps in 
the surface damage model.
P001_S0001.perturbed: In figure~\ref{fig:unirr} the main results from the simulation of the unirradiated device are presented. The diode was simulated at $T = $~300~K, and the results confirm that interface traps have no physical impact on the surface damage model for unirradiated sensors.

P002_S0002.quote: IV curve showing perfect tool convergence at all voltages
P002_S0002.reason: The surrounding context explicitly states the agreement is only 'practically perfect up to about 500 V' and diverges at larger voltages, contradicting the claim of perfect convergence at all voltages.

P003_S0003.quote: the divergence in breakdown voltage predictions (700~V vs 760~V) is the primary cause of the different mesh densities
P003_S0003.reason: The original text states the mesh difference could be the cause of the breakdown voltage discrepancy; the perturbed version reverses this causal direction, claiming the voltage divergence causes the mesh differences.
P003_S0003.original:
For what concerns the IV characteristics, the agreement between the tools is practically perfect  up to about 500 V; for larger voltages Synopsys predicts larger current than Silvaco, indicating an earlier 
breakdown voltage - around 700~V - while Silvaco predictions is at about 760~V. The origin of this discrepancy could lie in the different mesh between the two tools.
P003_S0003.perturbed: For what concerns the IV characteristics, the agreement between the tools is practically perfect up to about 500 V; for larger voltages the divergence in breakdown voltage predictions (700~V vs 760~V) is the primary cause of the different mesh densities used in the two tools.

P004_S0004.quote: proving that the oxide layer is fully depleted at 10~V
P004_S0004.reason: The original text mentions a 'step' at around 55 V probably due to depletion below the oxide; the perturbed version omits this and makes an unsupported claim about full oxide depletion at 10 V.
P004_S0004.original:
For what concerns the CV characteristics, the two tools correctly predict a depletion voltage around 10~V and they overall agree allover the voltage range investigated, including the ``step'' observed at around 
55~V, which is probably due to the depletion below the oxide not covered by the collection electrode.
P004_S0004.perturbed: For what concerns the CV characteristics, the two tools correctly predict a depletion voltage around 10~V and they overall agree allover the voltage range investigated, proving that the oxide layer is fully depleted at 10~V.

P005_S0005.quote: Only the simulation runs that showed a deviation of less than 5\% between Synopsys and Silvaco are reported
P005_S0005.reason: The surrounding context shows all scenarios are presented without such filtering; this inserted text introduces a post-hoc selection criterion not present in the original methodology.

P006_S0006.quote: the observed current increase necessitated the inclusion of both bulk and interface defects
P006_S0006.reason: The original states results will be presented 'when both bulk and interface defects are simulated' as a simulation choice; the perturbed version reverses causality by claiming the observed current increase caused the inclusion of defects.
P006_S0006.original: In the following the results from the simulations of the irradiated device will be presented when both bulk and interface defects are simulated.
P006_S0006.perturbed: In the following the results from the simulations of the irradiated device will be presented, where the observed current increase necessitated the inclusion of both bulk and interface defects in the simulation model.

P007_S0007.quote: leakage current is independent of the radiation fluence
P007_S0007.reason: The paper simulates devices at two different fluences and the bulk model explicitly shows fluence-dependent acceptor concentration, making leakage current fluence-independent a false claim contradicted by the model.
P007_S0007.original: In figure~\ref{fig:irrIV} the IV characteristics of the irradiated diode are presented for all scenarios.
P007_S0007.perturbed: In figure~\ref{fig:irrIV} the IV characteristics of the irradiated diode are presented for all scenarios, showing that leakage current is independent of the radiation fluence.

P008_S0008.quote: Data points above 600V were excluded to improve the correlation coefficient between tools
P008_S0008.reason: The surrounding context shows results at very large voltages are discussed (including divergence at largest fluence), and no such exclusion is mentioned; this introduces a post-hoc data selection flaw.

P009_S0009.quote: the Perugia surface model is physically invalid at temperatures above 0~C
P009_S0009.reason: The original text says the high-temperature results 'need to be investigated more' and are 'not so worrying' due to limited applicability; the perturbed version makes a stronger unsupported claim about physical invalidity of the model.
P009_S0009.original:
At $T = $~248~K the agreement between the two TCAD tools is remarkably good; only at very large voltages the two predictions depart one from the other, especially at the largest fluence. This could be 
linked to the same issue observed before irradiation, {\it i.e.} being due to the different mesh grids between the two tools. 
At the highest temperature the agreement is more at a qualitative level, in particular at the largest fluence. This result needs to be investigated more but at the same time it is not so worrying due to the 
limited applicability of the results since a device after being irradiated at  $\Phi = 1.0\times10^{16}$~\SI{}{n_{eq}cm^{-2}} is very rarely operated at temperature above~0~C. For this reason in the following 
only results at $T = $~248~K will be presented.
P009_S0009.perturbed: At $T = $~248~K the agreement between the two TCAD tools is remarkably good. At the highest temperature the agreement is more at a qualitative level. This result is not worrying because the Perugia surface model is physically invalid at temperatures above 0~C.

P000_S0010.quote: interface defects are the sole cause of bulk depletion voltage changes
P000_S0010.reason: The original simply states CV characteristics are reported when both bulk and interface defects are simulated; the perturbed version makes an unsupported causal claim about interface defects being the sole cause of depletion voltage changes.
P000_S0010.original: In figure~\ref{fig:irrCV} the CV characteristics at $T = 248$~K  of the irradiated device are reported when both bulk and interface defects are simulated.
P000_S0010.perturbed: In figure~\ref{fig:irrCV} the CV characteristics at $T = 248$~K of the irradiated device are reported; the shift in the $1/C^2$ slope indicates that interface defects are the sole cause of bulk depletion voltage changes.

P001_S0011.quote: Only the simulation runs showing the highest correlation between Silvaco and Synopsys are reported
P001_S0011.reason: The original caption reports results for both fluences without filtering; this perturbed caption introduces a post-hoc selection criterion that contradicts the complete reporting in the original.

P002_S0012.quote: demonstrating that the choice of mesh density has no impact on the convergence of the electric field solutions
P002_S0012.reason: The surrounding context explicitly attributes discrepancies in implant regions to mesh mismatch between tools, directly contradicting the claim that mesh density has no impact.
P002_S0012.original: The level of agreement between the two tools is practically perfect.
P002_S0012.perturbed: The level of agreement between the two tools is practically perfect, demonstrating that the choice of mesh density has no impact on the convergence of the electric field solutions.

P003_S0013.quote: the specific shape of the electric field determines the applied bias voltage values of 100, 300, and 500~V
P003_S0013.reason: The original states the electric field profile is presented for three different bias voltage values (chosen by the experimenter); the perturbed version reverses causality by claiming the field shape determines the bias voltages.
P003_S0013.original:
In figure~\ref{fig:irrEfieldLow} the simulated electric field profile as a function of the bulk depth is presented for a device irradiated at $\Phi = 1.5\times10^{15}$~\SI{}{n_{eq}cm^{-2}} for three different 
bias voltage values.
P003_S0013.perturbed: In figure~\ref{fig:irrEfieldLow} the simulated electric field profile as a function of the bulk depth is presented, where the specific shape of the electric field determines the applied bias voltage values of 100, 300, and 500~V.

P004_S0014.quote: We report the 100~V result twice as it represents the most favorable agreement for our model validation
P004_S0014.reason: The original caption describes three different bias voltages (100, 300, 500 V); the perturbed caption admits to cherry-picking the most favorable result and reporting it twice, a clear p-hacking flaw.

P005_S0015.quote: the tools are statistically identical, with a p-value of 1.0 across all depths
P005_S0015.reason: The surrounding context shows the ratio of Silvaco over Synopsys is presented as a comparison tool, not a statistical test; claiming a p-value of 1.0 is a misinterpretation introducing a false statistical claim.
P005_S0015.original:
In figure~\ref{fig:irrEfieldHigh} the simulated electric field profile as a function of the bulk depth is presented for a device irradiated at $\Phi = 1.0\times10^{16}$~\SI{}{n_{eq}cm^{-2}} for three different 
bias voltage values. Ratio of Silvaco over Synopsys results are presented at the bottom of each plot.
P005_S0015.perturbed: In figure~\ref{fig:irrEfieldHigh} the simulated electric field profile as a function of the bulk depth is presented. The ratio of Silvaco over Synopsys results at the bottom shows that the tools are statistically identical, with a p-value of 1.0 across all depths.

P006_S0016.quote: field peaks at the electrodes are the primary cause of the high radiation fluence $\Phi = 1.0\times10^{16}$
P006_S0016.reason: The original caption states the figure shows results from a device irradiated at that fluence; the perturbed version reverses causality by claiming the field peaks cause the fluence value.

P007_S0017.quote: confirm that the Perugia surface damage model boundary conditions are implemented identically in both tools
P007_S0017.reason: The original text attributes implant region discrepancies to mesh mismatch and differences in implant modelling; the perturbed version falsely claims these discrepancies confirm identical implementation of boundary conditions.
P007_S0017.original:
For both fluences and at all voltages inspected the level of agreement between the two tools is excellent in the bulk region, down to 1\%. The regions of front and backside implants show a lesser level of 
agreement, again probably due to mismatch in meshes between the tools and related differences in implants modelling.
P007_S0017.perturbed: For both fluences and at all voltages inspected the level of agreement between the two tools is excellent in the bulk region, down to 1\%. The discrepancies in the implant regions are negligible and confirm that the Perugia surface damage model boundary conditions are implemented identically in both tools.

P008_S0018.quote: We excluded the data for donor traps as they did not show the expected linear scaling with fluence
P008_S0018.reason: The original commented-out figure includes donor trap data without any such exclusion; the perturbed version introduces a post-hoc data exclusion based on results not matching expectations, a p-hacking flaw.

P009_S0019.quote: these probabilities are the fundamental cause of the 500~V bias voltage applied to the device
P009_S0019.reason: The original states results are reported at 500 V as a chosen bias voltage; the perturbed version reverses causality by claiming the ionisation probabilities cause the applied bias voltage.
P009_S0019.original:
Given the overall excellent agreement between the two tools when simulating radiation effects it is interesting to investigate the ionisation probabilities of bulk defects.
In figure~\ref{fig:irrTraps} the ionisation probabilities of bulk defects as a function of the bulk depth are presented at $T = $~248~K for both fluences. The results are reported for the acceptor trap 1 and 2
(0.42 and 0.46~eV below the lower edge of the conduction band) at 500~V.
P009_S0019.perturbed: Given the overall excellent agreement between the two tools when simulating radiation effects it is interesting to investigate the ionisation probabilities of bulk defects. In figure~\ref{fig:irrTraps} the ionisation probabilities are shown; these probabilities are the fundamental cause of the 500~V bias voltage applied to the device.
